Through lecture, small- and large-group discussions, debates, audiovisuals, guest speakers, and directed reading assignments, the focus will be on reality-oriented sexual behavior and attitudes; needs and interests of college students; positive satisfaction, obligations, and responsibilities to self and others; sexual deviance; influence of society; and changing mores, roles, and lifestyles.

title: Addiction & Violence/Fam
description: Prerequisites: WELL 150 (or WELL 101 ), or permission; and L&I 100 (or waiver), or GSTR 110 (or waiver) This course explores family dynamics from a family systems perspective and examines how those dynamics are affected by addiction and violence. Topics covered include: the development of family theories; the structure of a family system; the impact on children and their future relationships; the interplay between substance abuse and the family system; domestic violence; and trauma healing. Prevention, intervention, and treatment options for addictions will be emphasized.

title: Health Psychology (PSY)
description: Prerequisite: PSY 100 A course designed for Psychology majors, but with relevance for Nursing majors, Physical Education majors, pre-med and pre-dental students, and interested others. Interdisciplinary in content, the course examines how biological, psychological, and social factors interact with and effect health, chronic disease, health-promotion efforts, medical treatment, and stress management. Course content also will examine sound research principles, alternative or complementary treatments, and application techniques for health-habit changes. 1 Course Credit

title: Medical Terminology
description: HLT 205 is an introductory course in health studies that provides an understanding of anatomical, physiological, and pathological terminology with emphasis on work structures and definition of root words, suffixes, and prefixes from Greek and Latin.  Additional emphasis is placed on spelling and pronunciation. This course is primarily designed for individuals preparing for a career in health care.  No previous knowledge of Greek or Latin is required.  Noncredit for students who completed HLT 286 Medical Terminology

title: Women and Health (WGS)
description: Prerequisite: HLT 200 OR a college course in Women's Studies OR permission of instructor This course will help students understand women's health from a personal perspective and then connect that understanding to the health of women in other parts of the world. Emphasis will be on reproductive health, including pregnancy, birth, and breastfeeding, but we will also study issues such as environmental health, violence against women, and AIDS as a global health problem.

title: Comprehensive First Aid
description: A comprehensive course teaching students the latest knowledge and skills necessary in recognizing and responding appropriately, until more advanced medical care arrives, to a variety of medical emergencies such as stoppage of breathing, cardiac arrest, airway obstruction, severe bleeding, poisoning, fractures, burns, shock, heat or cold related emergencies, seizures and sudden illness. The material will be presented in a classroom setting utilizing lecture, hands-on practical labs, videos, quizzes, written exams, student presentations and practical exams. As an American Red Cross course, the student has the opportunity to earn Responding to Emergencies-First Aid and Adult, Child and Infant CPR with AED certification during the class with successful completion of the American Red Cross standardized written exams as well as demonstrated competency in all required skills. This certification is good for 2 years.

title: Senior Research Seminar
description: Prerequisite: HIS 200 , completion of a 300-level HIS course, and senior standing, or junior standing with permission of instructor This seminar integrates the previous study of History by focusing on historical interpretations of events and experiences that transcent regions, nations, and even any single civilization.  Students will engage in a major research project that will result in a project and a presentation.

title: HLT Program Planning & Eval
description: Pre-requisites: WELL 150 (or WELL 101 & 102); L&I 100 (or waiver) or GSTR 110 ; HLT 200 or permission from instructor. This course provides the background information and skills educators needed to assess needs, plan, implement, and evaluate health education and promotion programming in a variety of environments, including schools, hospitals, worksites, community and clinical settings.  It explores the psychological, social, and physical implications of health education and promotion. The major emphasis of this course is to develop interventions based in theory and best practices that teach and promote health. Focus is on needs assessment, systematic planning, delivery, and assessment of programming, creative teaching, introduction to philosophy/history of health education, and applications of certain priority heath topics at the community and public health levels.

title: Introduction to Japanese I
description: Introduction to Japanese as it is spoken in Japan today. Speaking and listening comprehension will be emphasized.

title: Peer Health Educ & Promotion
description: This course is designed to provide classroom and experiential training for students interested in learning about health issues that impact college students, and theoretical and evidence-based approaches to educating and promoting health among this target population. Emphasis is given to a peer-to-peer approach and students will become certified peer educators. Course assignments give students the opportunity to share their knowledge and practice skills with their fellow Berea College students. There are no pre-requisites for this course.  Meets Practical Reasoning Perspective. Noncredit for students who completed HLT 286 Peer Health Education and Promotion

title: Health in Appalachia (APS)
description: Prerequisites: WELL 150 or permission; and L&I 100 (or wavier) or GSTR 110 (or waiver) This course explores various dimensions of the environment that impact the health of individuals and the communities in which they live within the Appalachian region. Specific topics addressed include: education, religion, industrialization, economic development, access to health care, sexuality, domestic violence, race and ethnicity, and substance abuse. This course is designed for the non-professional interested in the health needs of all people living in the Appalachian region and will include directed readings, lectures, discussions, films, guest speakers, and field trips.

title: Intermediate Japanese
description: Prerequisite: JPN 103 with a grade of C or higher or permission of instructor Continued practice of reading, writing, listening, and speaking, fully integrated with culture.

title: American Red Cross Instructor
description: The purpose of this course will be to provide the student with the knowledge and skills necessary to obtain certification as an American Red Cross Adult and Pediatric CPR/AED and First Aid Instructor. If the student does not hold the required basic-level Adult and Pediatric CPR/AED and First Aid certification prior to enrolling in the class, he/she will complete it during the class. There are no prerequisites for this 1 credit course. Noncredit for students who completed HLT 286

title: Introduction to Latin I
description: The fundamentals of Latin, with readings of simple prose. Introduction to Roman culture.

title: Epidemiology
description: Prerequisites: MAT 104 or other statistics course approved by instructor This course will provide an overview of the basic epidemiological principles, concepts, methods, and applications. The course is divided into three sections. The first section covers history, and quantitative measures of disease frequency, association, and effects. The second section covers types of epidemiological study designs, and the third and final section covers practical application of epidemiological principles to screening communicable and non-communicable diseases. Emphasis will be on disease prevention and the role of health education in reducing risk factors and improving health. 1 Course Credit

title: Introduction to Japanese III
description: Prerequisite: JPN 102 with a grade of C or higher or permission of instructor Continued practice of reading, writing, listening, and speaking, fully integrated with culture.

title: Introduction to Japanese II
description: Prerequisite: JPN 101 with a grade of C or higher or permission of instructor Continued emphasis on Japanese oral/aural communication skills and an introduction to the Japanese written language.

title: Introduction to Latin III
description: Prerequisite: LAT 102 with a grade of C or higher, or permission of instructor Advanced study in language and Roman culture as seen through selected writings of the Golden Age.

title: American Law Principles
description: Prerequisite: L&I 100 (or waiver), or GSTR 110 (or waiver), or Permission of Instructor This course introduces students to the study of contemporary law systems in the United States. The course considers law as a mechanism used to structure human societies, address conflicts, effect social change, and regulate social, political, ethical/moral, and economic issues that arise for institutions, organizations, and individuals. In addition, students will become familiar with the distinctive practices of common law methodology.

title: Math Modeling Using Computer
description: This course provides an introduction to computers and to mathematical modeling of problems from the real world with an emphasis on using the computer as a flexible problem-solving, predictive, and exploratory tool. A selected computer-application package is studied in depth and used to explore a variety of applied examples. This computer package may vary from section to section. NOTE: Noncredit for students who completed or waived MAT 110 (no longer offered at Berea College), 115, or 135.

title: Elementary Algebra I
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 010 This course is designed for students who need to review and master the first half of the elementary algebra topics; solving linear equations and inequalities; negative exponents; scientific notation; operations with polynomials; factoring polynomials; and operations with rational expressions.  (1 Course Credit for term full-time/part-time status; Noncredit toward required credits to graduate)

title: Introduction to Latin II
description: Prerequisite: LAT 101 with a grade of C or higher, or permission of instructor Continued study in the fundamentals with readings and explorations in Roman culture.

title: Intro to Discrete Math
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 Discrete mathematics is the branch of mathematics that deals with arrangements of distinct objects. The course will focus on three basic questions related to these arrangements: the existence of such arrangements, the counting of such arrangements, and the selection of a 'best' arrangement based upon some criterion. Techniques from this branch of mathematics have application in a wide variety of practical everyday situations, such as how to find the best route for snowplows in a city or what voting method would best represent the will of the voters. Other useful applications include how to count the number of different possible combinations of color and type of a marketed product, and how best to schedule a list of tasks to be done. Discrete mathematical models are used by decision makers in our society, from workers in government to those in health care, transportation, and telecommunications. Non-credit for students who have completed MAT 216.

title: Environ Issue:Math Model Appr
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 The state of the planet will be discussed from differing points of view. Local and global issues such as air, land, and water pollution; energy production and consumption; forest, wetland, and other resource management; and waste disposal will be considered. Mathematical models will be developed and explored on the computer in order to make predictions and to consider solutions regarding the environmental problems that face our world. NOTE: Noncredit for students who completed or waived MAT 101, 110 (no longer offered at Berea College), 115, or 135.

title: Legal Reasoning & Advocacy II
description: A study of how Common Law methodology is applied to the resolution of major fundamental rights disputes in American Law. Students in this course will explore a complex case problem designed to simulate the appeal of a proceeding via a writ of certiorari to the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS). As in real arguments before the SCOTUS, students will learn the case law spanning a large number of real-world earlier written opinions issued by both the SCOTUS and other federal circuit courts of appeal and state supreme courts in the US legal system. As part of learning this legal foundation, students will have to draft and submit formal case summaries in preparation for presenting their cases. In addition, students will have to argue orally as both petitionerâs counsel and then alternatively as respondentâs counsel in various "practice panel" simulations of SCOTUS proceedings. NOTE: students must first take LES 370 as "Legal Reasoning and Advocacy I" for one full credit before taking LES 371 as "Legal Reasoning and Advocacy II" for one additional full credit. Noncredit for LES 370 for students who completed this course of study as LES 286, and noncredit for students who twice completed this course of study as both LES 286 and LES 386.

title: Trigonometry with Applications
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 115 with a "C" or better This course offers a study of trigonometry and trigonometric functions. Topics will include a review of basic concepts of functions and graphs; trigonometric functions; analytic trigonometry; trigonometric equations; and polar coordinates and vectors. Applications of trigonometry will be investigated through the development and analysis of mathematical models.

title: Legal Reasoning & Advocacy I
description: A study of how Common Law methodology is applied to the resolution of major fundamental rights disputes in American Law. Students in this course will explore a complex case problem designed to simulate the appeal of a proceeding via a writ of certiorari to the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS). As in real arguments before the SCOTUS, students will learn the case law spanning a large number of real-world earlier written opinions issued by both the SCOTUS and other federal circuit courts of appeal and state supreme courts in the US legal system. As part of learning this legal foundation, students will have to draft and submit formal case summaries in preparation for presenting their cases. In addition, students will have to argue orally as both petitionerâs counsel and then alternatively as respondentâs counsel in various "practice panel" simulations of SCOTUS proceedings. NOTE: students must first take LES 370 as "Legal Reasoning and Advocacy I" for one full credit before taking LES 371 as "Legal Reasoning and Advocacy II" for one additional full credit. Noncredit for LES 370 for students who completed this course of study as LES 286, and noncredit for students who twice completed this course of study as both LES 286 and LES 386.

title: Pre-Algebra
description: This course is designed for students who need to review and master pre-algebra topics including computation of whole numbers, decimals, fractions, and integers; ration, proportion, and percent; and geometry.  (1 Course Credit for term full-time/part-time status; Noncredit toward required credits to graduate)

title: Calculus I
description: Prerequisite: A knowledge of trigonometry and MAT 110, 112, or 125 with a grade of C or higher, or waiver of same based on placement test A study of analytic methods in geometry precedes an introduction to differential calculus. Main topics include exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions, limits, continuity, differentiation, indeterminate forms, and applications of differential calculus.

title: Elementary Algebra II
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 011 This course is the third and final course in the developmental-math sequence; consequently, the content consists of review of the material covered in the first two developmental math courses, reinforcing and expanding that material, and also covers linear graphing; solving linear systems of equations; basic problem solving; rational expressions and exponents; roots and radicals; and quadratic equations. Students will be exposed to proper study skills for learning mathematics and will participate in group learning activities. The course is designed to prepare students for subsequent courses with quantitative reasoning/mathematics content.  (1 Course Credit for term full-time/part-time status; Noncredit toward required credits to graduate)

title: Found/Tchg of Elem Math II
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012; MAT 201 with a grade of C or higher This course is a continuation of MAT 201. Topics in mathematics to be covered include: definitions, axioms, similarity, congruence, areas, and volumes. Throughout the course, the emphasis will be on understanding ideas in mathematics through problem solving and the use of manipulatives, and using this understanding to investigate and develop strategies for teaching elementary mathematics. Field experiences with children are required of all students enrolled.

title: Discrete Mathematics
description: Prerequisite: Waiver or completion of MAT 012 Discrete mathematics is the study of mathematical structures which may be described by the integers and which typically consider finite, distinctly separated values.  Selected topics for this course include: counting techniques, Boolean logic, set theory, mathematical induction and recursion with an emphasis on combinatorial graphs (aka networks).  Applications and mathematical ideas of particular use to computer science will be emphasized.  Non-credit for students who have completed MAT 415.

title: Introduction to Statistics
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 This course is designed for students with little mathematics background who wish to gain experience with the basic concepts of statistics and their applications. Topics include graphic representations of data; measures of central tendency and variability; probability; sampling distributions; estimation using confidence intervals; and significance testing. NOTE: This course is not intended for Mathematics majors and is noncredit for students who completed ECO 250 or MAT 311 .

title: College Algebra with Modeling
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 This course offers a study of algebraic functions and their uses in mathematical modeling. Topics will include the real-number system; basic concepts of functions and graphs; linear, polynomial, rational, exponential, and logarithmic functions and models; and the solutions of systems of linear equations. Applications to various disciplines will be investigated through the development and analysis of mathematical models. NOTE: Noncredit for students who completed or waived MAT 135.

title: Teaching of Mathematics
description: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor This course is designed to acquaint prospective secondary-school teachers with the issues affecting the teaching of mathematics including theory, research, content, and methodology; the resources available to assist them in teaching mathematics; and the NCTM's Professional Standards. Particular emphasis will be given to the use of manipulatives, technology, problem solving, and cooperative learning in the teaching of mathematics. Observing and teaching youths are required of all students enrolled.

title: Probability
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 with a grade of C or higher; MAT 225 or concurrent enrollment; or permission of instructor The goal of this course is to introduce students to the fundamental concepts of Probability theory and its applications.  Topics include:  random experiments, outcomes, and events; conditional probability and independence; random variables; discrete and continuous distributions; moment generating functions; jointly distributed random variables; and the Central Limit Theorem.

title: Foundations of Geometry
description: Prerequisite: MAT 315 This course will include an overall view of the structure of geometry evolving from the basic axioms of Euclidean geometry. The interrelationships between various geometries such as affine, neutral, hyperbolic, projective, elliptic, and others will be studied, as well as some of the easier, important results of each. Because this material is necessary for a good understanding of Euclidean geometry, it is important that those students considering a high-school teaching career in mathematics enroll in this course.

title: Geometry-Midl Grades/Elem Tchr
description: Prerequisite:  A grade of C or higher in MAT 201. A study of geometric concepts, mathematical justification, and methods for teaching geometry to elementary and middle grade students.  The geometric concepts include: the properties of lines, triangles, quadrilaterals, circles, tessellations and geometric solids.  These concepts will be explored through problem solving, manipulatives, educational software and cooperative learning.

title: Calculus III
description: Prerequisite: MAT 225 with a grade of C or higher This course is a continuation of MAT 225. Main topics include three dimensional vectors, space, curves, solid analytic geometry, differential calculus of several variables, and multiple integration. Microcomputer graphics and computational packages will be introduced and used in the analysis of selected problems.

title: Found/Tchg Elementary Math I
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 This course will be a study of basic ideas of sets, operations on sets, the counting numbers, and the extension of the numbers to include the rational and real number systems. Throughout the course, the emphasis will be on understanding ideas in mathematics through problem solving and the use of manipulatives, and using this understanding to investigate and develop strategies for teaching elementary mathematics. Field experiences with children are required for all students enrolled.

title: Differential Equations
description: Prerequisite: MAT 225 This course provides an introduction to both pure and applied aspects of differential equations. Topics to be studied include first-order equations, second-order linear equations, power-series solutions, Laplace Transforms, systems of first-order equations, and nonlinear equations.

title: Linear Algebra
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 or permission of instructor This course illustrates the nature of mathematics as a blend of techniques, theory, abstraction, and applications.  The problem of solving linear equations leads to the algebra of matrices, determinants, vector spaces, bases and dimensions, linear transformations and eigenvalues.

title: Operations Research
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 or permission of instructor Operations Research is an applied area employing both mathematics and computer science in addressing certain kinds of decision problems arising in a wide variety of disciplinary and professional contexts. The goals of the course will be to develop skills in identifying typical problems; formulating, solving, and interpreting appropriate models; and developing interactive feedback with the problem environment. Topics include linear programming and network formulations; the simplex algorithm and its computer implementation; sensitivity analysis; duality; network algorithms; and dynamic programming.

title: Calculus II
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 with a grade of C or higher This course is a continuation of MAT 135. Main topics include the definite integral, applications of the definite integral, integration techniques, numerical integration, and series. Microcomputer graphics and computational packages will be introduced and used in the analysis of selected problems.

title: Fundamental Concepts of Math
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 Designed to acquaint students with some of the concepts and methods fundamental to all areas of mathematics. Topics will include set theory, relations, functions, logic, methods of proof, cardinality, and selected properties of the real number system.

title: Numerical Analysis (CSC)
description: Prerequisite: MAT 225; one course chosen from CSC 111 , 114, 125, 126, 226; AND one course chosen from MAT 214, 315, or 330 This course is designed for students who are concerned with the development of approximation methods and their use in locating roots of equations, interpolation, numerical differentiation, numerical integrations, and solution of systems of linear equations.

title: Math Lit: Rdg & Communication
description: Prerequisite: Senior standing and permission of instructor The main goal of this course is to introduce students to research and communication in mathematics. Teams of students will work together reading and discussing selected articles from selected mathematical journals, writing team papers, and giving team presentations on the material read. The emphasis will be placed on developing reading comprehension of mathematics above the textbook level and on developing effective methods for communicating this information. The course will culminate in the completion of individual student projects, with students selecting their own article or topic to research, and presenting the material both in a formal research paper and in an oral presentation. The satisfactory completion of this individual project will satisfy the senior seminar requirement for the Mathematics Major.

title: Senior Mathematics Seminar
description: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor and senior standing During the senior year, each Mathematics major is required to register for this course. Each studentâs project will begin with a lead paper provided by a faculty-resource person. The project will involve preparation of a 4-10 page research paper and will conclude with an oral presentation. Successful completion of this course will satisfy the majorâs field-writing requirement.

title: Combinatorics
description: Discrete Mathematics is a branch of mathematics concerning countable discrete structures. Discrete problems arise in many different areas of pure mathematics ranging from abstract algebra to probability theory, but there are many applied uses of combinatorial knowledge in fields such as computer science or physics. The goals of the course will be to develop skills in identifying typical problems and formulating, solving, and interpreting appropriate models. Topics of the course will include the Pigeonhole Principle, Ramsey Numbers, Algorithms (Greedy, Breadth-First, Depth-First, Brute Force), recurrence relations, Graph Theory (graphs, digraphs, trees, bipartite graphs, matchings, coloring, graph isomorphisms, planarity), multisets, compositions, permutations, integer partitions, ordinary and exponential generating functions, the Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion, and other topics as time or student interest permits.

title: Class Piano
description: Designed for the College student who has had little or no formal keyboard instruction. Emphasis on reading and music fundamentals, and functional use of the piano. Two periods per week. Course Fee: $10 per term.

title: Class Voice
description: First year of vocal study; open to all Berea College students. The class will meet as a whole two hours per week. In addition, students will be divided into smaller groups to rehearse with an accompanist. Some individual practice also is required. All students will have an opportunity to perform at the weekly performance class. Course Fee: $10 per term.

title: Abstract Algebra
description: Prerequisite: MAT 315 and 330 or permission of instructor A course designed to introduce students to the methods and topics essential to the study of algebraic structure and its implications. An introduction to group theory will serve to launch an investigation of more highly structured algebras such as rings, integral domains, and fields.

title: Real Analysis
description: Prerequisite: MAT 315 and 330, or permission of instructor Basic algebraic and topological properties of the real number system will be established and then applied to the study of such concepts as limit, continuity, differentiation, integration, and infinite series.

title: Topology
description: Prerequisite: MAT 315 and 330, or permission of instructor Topological spaces will be approached by abstracting from a preliminary study of metric spaces. Topics in metric and/or topological settings include open and closed sets; open base and subbase; first and second countability; dense sets; continuity; metrizability; and compactness, connectedness, and separation properties.

title: Fundamentals of Music
description: An introduction to reading music, including musical symbols and terminology, rhythm and meter, scales, key signature, intervals, and chords.  Both written and aural skills are developed.  Simple musical forms and music literature are included.  Designed particularly for: perspective Music majors and minors who need further development of fundamental written and/or aural skills in music in preparation for courses in the Materials of Music and Aural Harmony sequence.  Two to three class periods per week  This course also is open to the general college student who wishes to develop fundamental knowledge and skills in music.

title: Statistics
description: Prerequisite: MAT 311 with a grade of C or higher and MAT 330 or concurrent enrollment A continuation of MAT 311 . The student should gain an appreciation of the nature, scope, and theoretical basis of methods of statistical inference. Topics will include estimation, hypotheses testing, and linear regression. Applications will be discussed.

title: Intro to Music Literature
description: An introduction to the study of music literature. Students in this course will be introduced to significant musical pieces from the major historical periods and will learn to distinguish the musical stylistic characteristics of those periods. Emphasis will be on the development of listening skills and appropriate methods and abilities to analyze, interpret, discuss, and write about music. May include required attendance of live musical performances on campus or in the region, as available and appropriate. Four periods per week.

title: Bluegrass Music Ensemble
description: Prerequisite: Students must have previous performance experience before enrolling in the Bluegrass Ensemble. All students must meet with the instructor for an informal audition. An ensemble of up to six members (guitar, banjo, fiddle, mandolin, bass, and lead singer). The ensemble specializes in traditional and contemporary bluegrass and old-time music. The group performs on and off campus.

title: Contemp Percussion Ensemble
description: An ensemble that includes drums, percussion, and a basic modern-rhythm section. The ensemble performs a range of contemporary styles including jazz, Latin, rock, funk, and world beat.

title: Complex Analysis
description: Prerequisite: MAT 330 The main objective will be to study functions of a complex variable. The study will draw heavily on the student's previous experience with functions of a real variable. Topics will include the complex numbers differentiation and integration of functions of a complex variable; power series representation; analytic functions; and the calculus of residues.

title: World Music
description: This course will examine the music within four cultures: Native American, Indian, African, and African-American. The music of each culture will be examined within the context of its history, peoples, and traditions. Musical examples, videos, oral presentations, class demonstrations, and special programs/concerts will be included. Students also will attend several College convocations and discuss, compare, and reflect on the various music cultures and experiences.

title: Folk Roots Ensemble
description: The Berea College Folk-Roots Ensemble plays Appalachian music in all its forms and branches out into other kinds of folk and roots music from there.  Founded in 2012, the group has played with the Chieftains, opened for Grammy winners Vince Gill, Guy Davis, and Tom Paxton, and does regular tours of the Southeastern United States. Singers and instrumentalists (guitar, banjo, fiddle, mandolin, piano, bass, dulcimer, accordion, etc.) of all levels welcome.  Open to all Berea College students.

title: Chamber Singers
description: A select 10- to 20-voice ensemble chosen by audition from the Concert Choir. The Chamber Singers perform at all Concert Choir appearances, sing off-campus in the region, and perform at College chapel services.

title: Jazz Ensemble
description: An instrumental ensemble that performs various styles of jazz for big band (swing, blues, bebop, and other types of jazz), including traditional cool, Latin, and jazz-rock fusion. The group performs on campus and throughout the region. This course includes a spring tour, and students will be expected to pay some portion of the costs. The fees will be announced on the first day of class and included in the syllabus. Students also may have to cover the cost of formal footwear.

title: Mariachi Ensemble
description: Mariachi Berea was established in 2015, to expand representation of and exposure to the musical heritage of Hispanic culture, the fastest growing demographic in Appalachia. Members play one or more instruments in the band comprising of trumpets, violins, and a rhythm section of guitar, vihuela (small 5-string Mexican guitar) and guitarrÃ³n, the traditional mariachi bass instrument.

title: Black Music Ensemble
description: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor * Open to all Berea students, this ensemble specializes in the performance of African-American sacred music, particularly spirituals and gospel music. The ensemble meets twice weekly for one-and-one-half hours. Regular attendance is required at all rehearsals and performances including annual Fall and Spring concerts, as well as several other programs each term. Membership by permission of instructor after audition. May be repeated for credit. Course Fee: $15 (for transportation and robe cleaning).

title: African-Latin Percussion Ensmb
description: An ensemble that performs a variety of rhythms based on the traditional drumming styles of the Caribbean, West Africa, and South America.

title: Bel Canto Treble Singers
description: An auditioned ensemble open to all Berea College students capable of singing in the Soprano and/or Alto (Treble) range.  The choir performs a wide variety of choral music for treble voices, including sacred, secular, Broadway, and folk genres. Performances are at campus and community functions with several short, off-campus trips.

title: Materials of Music II
description: Prerequisite: MUS 220 (formerly 120) Continuation of MUS 220. The study of non-harmonic tones, seventh chords, modulation, the four-part chorale style, and keyboard style. Music forms, introductory counterpoint, and analysis of works from music literature. Playing basic chord progressions using the secondary as well as primary triads, harmonizing simple melodies, simple transposition, and basic harmonic progressions in four parts. Two class periods per week plus a keyboard lab. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 223 (formerly 313).

title: Aural Harmony I
description: Prerequisite: MUS 118 or passing Music Theory Placement Exam The relationship between the printed musical symbol and its auditory representation. The 'musical ear' is developed through the sight-singing of simple melodies, intervals, scales, and rhythmic patterns. Brief exercises in melodic and rhythmic dictation and conducting patterns. Three periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 220

title: American Popular Music
description: Prior musical experience not requisite, but welcome. What is American popular music? What does the common definition include? What does it exclude? This course examines different genres of American popular music and traces the multicultural and multi-ethnic roots of these styles. Activities involve active listening, analysis, reading, watching films and videos, conducting research, possibly writing and performing songs, and writing a final research paper. We will be examining Country, Rap and Hip-Hop, Rock, R&B, Black Gospel, Bluegrass, The Great American Songbook, Singer-Songwriters, Folk, Italian-American, Latin, and Caribbean music, and exploring their shared and differing roots.

title: Concert Choir
description: A mixed-choral ensemble open to all Berea College students which performs a wide variety of musical styles, both sacred and secular, and in several languages. Regular attendance at all rehearsals and performances is required. Four periods per week. May be repeated for credit. Course Fee: $25 (for Fall Term enrollments).

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Open to all Berea College students; membership is gained by audition and demonstrated ability to sight read. Regular attendance at all rehearsals and performances is required. Meets three times weekly, performs at several College functions and in concert. Students may have to cover the costs of formal footwear. Membership in Wind Ensemble is a prerequisite to Stage Band membership. May be repeated for credit.

title: Afri-Amer Mus:Overview
description: Prerequisite: L&I 100 (or waiver) or GSTR 110 (or waiver) The study of African-American music and musicians within the general framework of American society, tracing the development of the music from its African origins to its impact upon and fusion with American music and culture. Although the main focus will be placed upon music indigenous to African-American culture, study will encompass significant African-American musicians in the European classical music tradition.

title: Survey of Amer Music Thr (THR)
description: This course provides an overview of productions form American Musical theatre from the 19th century minstrelsy through present-day Broadway musicals. Students will be given the opportunity evaluate examples of musical theatre according to their historical, cultural, and social contexts. Students will identify composers, lyricists, directors, producers, choreographers, and performing artists that have contributed to the development of musical theatre and develop skills to evaluate performances and form their own opinions. Please note: If you took Introduction to Musical Theatre/Survey of Musical Theatre as Special Topics MUS186 or MUS286, you are ineligible for this course.

title: Materials of Music III
description: Prerequisite: MUS 222 Continuation of MUS 222. Seventh chords, altered chords, chromatic harmony, modulation, musical forms, and analysis of musical examples from the common practice period. Three periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 321.

title: Materials of Music I
description: Prerequisite: MUS 118 or passing Music Theory Placement Exam Beginning a four-term sequence of courses in the theoretical approach to music, this is a study of the properties of sound, the overtone series, symbols of notation, rhythm, intervals, scales, and key signatures. Harmonic studies include triads and their inversions, phrase structure and cadences, and harmonic progressions. The course will focus on playing major and minor scales, all triad types and their inversions, and basic chord progressions using primary triads. Two class periods per week plus a keyboard lab. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 221. NOTE: Noncredit for students who completed MUS 120.

title: Appalachian Music (APS)
description: An introduction to the music that has been, and is, an important part of the culture of the Southern Appalachian region. Specific musical elements will be discussed in relation to ballads and songs, instrumental music, bluegrass, country, and various types of religious music. The importance of the region's music and musicians in the development of country and popular music in America is treated. Three periods per week.

title: Aural Harmony II
description: Prerequisite: MUS 221 Continuation of MUS 221. Further studies in sight-singing; melodic and rhythmic dictation; harmonic backgrounds and part-singing; syncopation; and beginning harmonic dictation. Three periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 322.

title: Aural & Keyboard Harmony IV
description: Prerequisite: MUS 321 Continuation of MUS 321. Sight-singing of modulating; modal, tonal, and atonal melodies; and melodic, contrapuntal and harmonic dictation. Continuation of computer usage in furthering aural skills. Keyboard skills include harmonization using diatonic and altered chords, transposition, chord symbols, improvisation, and figured bass. Preparation for Piano Proficiency Examination. Two periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 322.

title: Tchg Brass & Percussion Instru
description: Prerequisite: Admission to Music or Music Education major; or permission of instructor A study of the materials, methods, literature, and pedagogy of brass and percussion instruments. Course requirements include reading and research assignments, examination of appropriate materials and equipment, a performance and teaching laboratory component, and practicum experiences. Three class meetings per week.

title: Mat & Meth of Elem Music
description: Prerequisite: Admission for the Music or Music Education major; or permission of the instructor. MUS 335 is an introduction to philosophies, methodologies, and pedagogical foundations of teaching music in elementary schools, including, but not limited to, the music teaching philosophies of Kodaly, Orff, Dalcroze, and Gordon. Course activities will include review of materials, assigned readings, research, class discussions, practical teaching laboratory sessions, and practicum experiences. Assessment, planning, and implementation of music education activities for people with unique needs and abilities will be covered Special attention is directed to preparing performance evidence of addressing New Teacher Standards I-IV.  Four meetings per week.

title: Fund Tchg Instrumental Music
description: Prerequisites: Music Majors only or by permission of the instructor; EDS 150 and MUS 223 This course provides an overview of the instrumental music education profession, including the philosophy, methodology, and pedagogical foundations that are essential for successful teaching. Students will be directed in their development of musical teaching-assessment, administrative, and personal, skills that are necessary for successful teaching. Assessment, planning, and implementation of music education activities for people with unique needs and abilities will be covered Special attention is directed to preparing performance evidence of addressing New Teacher Standards I-IV. Four periods per week.

title: Aural & Keyboard Harmony III
description: Prerequisite: MUS 223 Continuation of MUS 223. Sight-singing of diatonic and chromatic melodies; use of computers in developing aural skills; melodic, rhythmic, contrapuntal, and harmonic dictation. Harmonizing melodies at the piano keyboard. Two periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 320.

title: Materials of Music IV
description: Prerequisite: MUS 320 Continuation of MUS 320. Modulation to remote keys, chords of multiple thirds, larger musical forms, alternatives to traditional harmony, impressionism atonality, polytonality, serialism, electronic music, minimalism, and new systems of notation. Analysis of late 19th- and 20th-century works. Creative writing in various styles. Three periods per week. Usually taken in conjunction with MUS 323.

title: Conducting
description: Prerequisite: MUS 223 Study and practice of beat patterns, baton technique, left-hand functions, cueing, pulse changes, recitative conducting, error detection, score reading, score marking, and other techniques used in instrumental and choral conducting. Practical experience. Concurrent participation in ensemble of major applied instrument (MUS 131, 132, or 135) is required for laboratory experience. Three periods per week.

title: Piano Pedagogy
description: Prerequisite: MUA 221p (piano) or permission of instructor Lectures and discussion of teaching skills on all levels, in addition to a survey of methods and materials. Private and class teaching methods. Two periods per week.

title: Music Composition
description: Selected topics in composition are studied, such as texture; tonal and serial procedures; melody with accompaniment; and idiomatic writing for instruments and voices. Students use skills acquired through previous study of theory, harmony, and musical analysis in arranging and creating pieces in small and large forms. A weekly seminar (one hour) and individual conferences (one-half hour) focus on the creation of original works by students and detailed analysis of works that exemplify the selected topics. Students also will gain familiarity with computer programs in music notation and publishing. Individual assignments are made on the basis of each studentâs ability, training, and experience. May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor.

title: Music History I
description: Prerequisites: Declared Music or Music Education major or Music minor; and MUS 115, MUS 222; or permission of instructor An upper-level historical survey of Western music - its styles, genres, composers, and works. A 'music-centered' course, this survey will include analysis of representative works of the music literature. The music of classical Greece and the early Christian era through the Baroque, first term; the Classic era to the present day, second term. Four periods per week.

title: Teaching Woodwind Instruments
description: Prerequisite: Admission to Music or Music Education major; OR permission of instructor A study of the materials, literature, and pedagogy of woodwind instruments. Course requirements include reading and research assignments, examination of appropriate materials and equipment, a performance and teaching laboratory component, and practicum experiences. Three class meetings per week.

title: Music History II
description: Prerequisites: Declared Music or Music Education major or Music minor; and MUS 115, MUS 222; or permission of instructor An upper-level historical survey of Western music - its styles, genres, composers, and works. A 'music-centered' course, this survey will include analysis of representative works of the music literature. The music of classical Greece and the early Christian era through the Baroque, first term; the Classic era to the present day, second term. Four periods per week.

title: Foundations Nursing Practice
description: This course describes professional identity in nursing, nursing practice, and hands-on bedside care, including safety protocols, communication, vital signs measurement, and other entry-level skills.  By the end of the course, students will develop a personal, professional identity as a novice nursing student, and demonstrate basic skill competency in preparation of providing safe, person-centered bedside care.  This course is required for all in-coming nursing students.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Prerequisite: Senior standing and permission of instructor Senior Seminar is designed to be the capstone experience of the Music Program's core curriculum. As such, the seminar will focus on the research, study, practice, and implementation of the senior Music major's final project, which will consist of a final paper, and a performance or other public presentation. The seminar will function as a laboratory for students to present ideas and receive feedback as they work collaboratively and individually in completing their projects. As a culmination and integration of training and learning and as preparation for their professional future, this seminar provides an important support structure and learning community for Music majors. Course will meet once per week. NOTE: Required of all General Music majors, and part of an optional course sequence for Music Education majors. Students in General Music may take MUS 400 (1/2 credit) and MUA 400 (1/2 credit) to fulfill the Senior Seminar requirement if they do a recital and pass a recital preview. Other General Music majors will take MUS 400 as a one-credit course. Music Education majors may choose to take the course as 1/2 or full credit, in consultation with their Academic Advisor.

title: Women's Hlth & Maternity Nurs
description: Prerequisites: Admission to the Nursing Major This course is designed to assist students in gaining the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to meet the healthcare needs of women across the lifespan. This course addresses womenâs health from puberty through the post-menopausal period; maternity care including pre-conception care, fetal development, normal and at-risk antepartum, intrapartum, postpartum, and neonatal nursing care. Active learning strategies such as simulation and hospital-based clinical experiences provide the students the opportunity to engage in hands-on experiences that reinforce didactic instruction. This course includes three didactic hours per week and 84 clinical hours per term.

title: Adult Health Nursing I
description: Prerequisites: NUR 301 and NUR 351 with a C or better Corequisites: NUR 352 and NUR 355 This course is the first of two adult health nursing courses. The course is designed to assist students in gaining the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to meet the healthcare needs of acutely ill adults and geriatric patients experiencing alterations in health. Physiological body systems are used as the organizing structure for the course. Emphasis is placed on the use of the nursing process to guide clinical reasoning for the provision of safe patient centered nursing care in accordance with standards and competencies of professional nursing practice. The clinical component provides the opportunity for students to enhance clinical reasoning, interprofessional communication, and collaboration with a health care team providing care to acutely ill adult and geriatric patients. This course includes five hours of didactic/classroom experience each week and 84 hours of clinical experience each term.  Additional clinical learning includes simulation experiences.

title: Voice Pedagogy & Diction
description: Prerequisite: MUA 326v (voice) or permission of instructors A study of the physiological functions involved in singing (breathing, phonation, etc.). Consideration of vocal methods, use of imagery in teaching, resonance, tone quality, placement, vocalization, diction (Italian, German, French, and English) including a working knowledge of the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA), and interpretation. There will be many reading assignments, as well as listening to recordings and observing singers. Practical experience will be obtained by working with members of the voice class. Three periods per week: one day will be a laboratory experience.

title: Piano Literature
description: Prerequisite: MUA 221p (piano) or permission of instructor Offered: Typically as student interest and faculty availability allow The study of the history and literature of piano music as it developed through the ages. Starting with the early national schools of keyboard music and progressing through the music of Beethoven, the course will deal with different phases of keyboard development, the more important musical forms, performance practice, and the significant piano music of the major composers of each era. Individual research, some informal performance in class by both the teacher and the student, as well as outside listening, will be included. Two periods per week.

title: Mental Health Nursing
description: Prerequisites: Admission to the Nursing major The focus of this course is on nursing care of clients living with psychiatric/mental health needs across the lifespan. Complex communication skills are developed for interacting across the health care continuum with mental health clients. This course includes three hours of didactic/classroom experience each week and 84 hours of clinical experience per term. Course Fee: $47, plus cost of materials

title: Intro to Nursing Profession
description: This course introduces the student to concepts essential to the nursing profession. Concepts central to the American Association of Colleges of Nursingâs (AACN) Essentials of Baccalaureate Education for Professional Nursing Practice and the Quality and Safety Education for Nurses (QSEN) Competencies will be included in the course. Additional foci for the course include professional ethics, nursing history, clinical reasoning and the nursing process, and therapeutic communication. This course is a two-hour hour didactic class per week. (0.5 Credit)

title: Community Health Nursing
description: Prerequisites: NUR 350, NUR 352 and NUR 355 with C or better. This course focuses on integration and application of public-health and community based nursing care. Social, cultural, economic, and political influences on families, communities, and the healthcare system will be explored. Emphasis is on assessment and health planning with families and populations at risk for health problems. The community health clinical settings include a variety of health and community agencies. This course includes three hours of didactic/classroom experience each week and 84 hours of clinical experience per term. Course Fee: $40, plus cost of materials.

title: Research in Nursing
description: Prerequisites:  MAT 104, NUR 301 and NUR 351 with C or better. This course focuses on critique of nursing research and application of research findings to contemporary practice and education. The course examines steps of the research process, related legal and ethical issues, and strategies for developing an evidence based practice. Students will engage in structured critique of qualitative and quantitative research and use of nursing knowledge as the basis for developing and maintaining an evidence based practice.  This course is a two hour didactic class per week.  (0.5 Credit) NOTE: Taken Fall Term of junior year.

title: Essentials Nursing Practice II
description: Prerequisites: BIO 201 , BIO 202 , NUR 110 , NUR 201 and NUR 225 with a grade of C or higher. Co-requisites: BIO 207 , CFS 221 , NUR 230 This course focuses on assessment of the health history and health status of adult and geriatric clients. Health promotion for adults and geriatric clients is included for each of the physical assessment areas. Students will be provided opportunities for using these health assessment techniques to guide nursing care in a clinical setting. This clinical experience will allow students to use previously learned nursing skills and professional standards when planning and providing care for adults and geriatric clients. This course includes five hours of classroom/didactic experience each week and 84 hours in an acute care clinical nursing experience. This course meets the Active Learning Experience (ALE) requirement.

title: Pharmacology
description: Prerequisites: BIO 201 , BIO 202 , NUR 110 , NUR 201 and NUR 225 with grade of C or better Co-requisite: NUR 226 The focus of this course is drug safety and the role of pharmacology in nursing practice. The emphasis is on the general principles of drug action that form the basis for understanding the actions of specific drugs. Upon completion of the course, students will demonstrate a basic understanding of pharmacology, drug safety, client education, and be able to apply the knowledge of pharmacologic basics to nursing practice. This course includes four hours in class per week.

title: Essentials Nursing Practice I
description: Prerequisites: BIO 201 , PSY 100, CHM 113 , and NUR 110 with a grade of C or higher.  Pre or co-requisite: BIO 202 (with a grade of C or higher) This course focuses on the safe performance of essential nursing skills required for professional practice. Students will be provided opportunities to develop and demonstrate competencies related to these psychomotor skills in the nursing skills laboratory. This course includes three hours of classroom experience and 3 hours of nursing skills laboratory exposure each week. Course Fee: $150.43, plus cost of materials.

title: Nurs Synthesis & Complex Care
description: Prerequisites: NUR 400 and NUR 448 with a grade of C or better Corequisite: NUR 449 This capstone course focuses on three areas: managing care for patients with multiple complex health problems, transitioning to practice settings, and preparing for the NCLEX-RN. A total of 210 clinical hours are required and students work one-on-one with assigned registered nurse preceptors in a variety of acute care settings. This course provides students opportunities to discuss issues related to the transition from the student role to an entry level graduate nurse position functioning as a generalist.

title: Morality, Law & Philosophy
description: An introductory course in philosophy through examination of contemporary moral and legal controversies, such as abortion, censorship, freedom of religion, equality, and civil disobedience, among others. Attention to classic and contemporary philosophic texts, relevant legal materials, and fundamental features of law and of morality. Practical Reasoning (PR).

title: Thinking About God
description: Prerequisite: L&I 200 (or waiver) or GSTR 210 An exploration of topics in the philosophy of religion. Possible topics include arguments for and against the existence of God, the nature and attributes of a divine being, and the nature of faith and religious experience.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 A study of deductive systems, including sentential and predicate logic. Attention to syntax, semantics, and derivations in formal languages.

title: Pediatric Nursing
description: Prerequisites:  NUR 301 and NUR 351 with a C or better. The foundation for this course is normal growth and development and common health alterations in pediatric clients. Clinical experience supports patient-centered care for pediatric clients and their families. This course includes three hours of didactic/classroom experience each week and 84 hours of clinical experience per term.

title: Adult Health Nursing II
description: Prerequisites:  NUR 350 and NUR 355 with a C or better This is the second of two adult health nursing courses. The course is designed to assist students in gaining the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to meet the healthcare needs of critically ill adults and geriatric patients experiencing alterations in health. Physiological body systems are used as the organizing structure for the course. Emphasis is placed on the use of the nursing process to guide clinical reasoning for the provision of safe patient centered nursing care in accordance with standards and competencies of professional nursing practice. The clinical component provides the opportunity for students to enhance clinical reasoning, interprofessional communication, and collaboration with a health care team providing care to critically ill adult and geriatric patients. Additional clinical learning includes simulation experiences.  This course includes five hours of didactic/classroom experience each week and 84 hours of clinical experience per term.

title: Medical Ethics
description: A survey of some crucial ethical issues as they relate to the fields of medicine and medical research. The topics covered will typically include: major ethical theories which justify various positions in medical ethics; ethical issues surrounding the beginning and end of life (abortion and euthanasia); and issues crucial to the medical professional/patient relationship.

title: Nursing Leadership/Management
description: Prerequisites:  NUR 400 and NUR 448 with grade of C or better Corequisite:  NUR 450 This course develops studentsâ knowledge, skills and attitudes in nursing leadership and management. It focuses on an assessment of personal leadership traits, theories and skills of leadership and management, interprofessional core competencies, and career readiness. This course includes four hours of didactic/classroom experience each week.

title: Introductory Reasoning
description: A course about principles and applications of correct canons of reasoning. Focus on identifying, evaluating, and constructing arguments using a variety of techniques.

title: Mind, Language, and World
description: An introduction to topics in metaphysics and epistemology. Topics covered might include the existence of God, the possibility of life after death, free will, perception and the external world, and personal identity.

title: Reflecting on Nature
description: Prerequisite: L&I 200 (or waiver) or GSTR 210 or sophomore standing A course on philosophically informed thinking about the environment. Course explores ways of thinking about the environment, humanity, and other species, focusing on a few environmental problems illustrating fundamental puzzles about the biological sciences, about our responsibilities for nature and for future generations, and about the basis for valuing things human and non-human, living and inanimate.

title: Philosophy of Art
description: An introduction to philosophical issues that arise in the arts. Topics to be covered include creativity and fidelity in production of art; beauty, ugliness, and the aesthetic experience; the nature of art and artwork; meaning and interpretation; and the relationship between art and other values.

title: Approaches to Ethics
description: Prerequisite: L&I 200 (or waiver) or GSTR 210 , or sophomore standing An examination of distinctive religious and philosophic ways of thinking about good, duty, virtue, and the nature of ethics. Attention to selected works of Aristotle, Kant, John Stuart Mill, and others.

title: Classical Philosophy
description: Prerequisite: One PHI course An examination of philosophy in ancient Greece and the Roman world - texts, ideas, and figures. Emphasis is placed on the epistemological and metaphysical views of Plato and Aristotle.

title: Philosophy in World Traditions
description: An introduction to philosophy from a comparative perspective, pulling on different world traditions' approaches and perspectives to investigate and to understand better both the complexities of such problems and the solutions offered by different approaches. Issues in metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, logic, the philosophy of mind, the philosophy of language, and aesthetics may be explored, and traditions including African, Asian, Indigenous, Islamic and Western will be incorporated. May be repeated for credit, provided topics differ.

title: Science, Technology & Ethics
description: An introduction to the philosophy of science and the ethical and moral issues to which science and technology give rise. Topics to be covered may include the structure of science, the moral limits of genetic research, the use of animals (both human and non-human) in scientific research, and issues of privacy and data gathering in computing technology.

title: Political Philosophy (PSC)
description: An introduction to issues in political philosophy. Beginning with some historical background, the course will concentrate on 20th - and 21st-century movements in political philosophy, concentrating on the work of John Rawls and Robert Nozick.

title: Philosophy: Descartes to Kant
description: Prerequisite: One PHI course An examination of European philosophy in the 17th and 18th centuries. Emphasis on the epistemological and metaphysical views of Descartes, Hume, Kant, and Locke.

title: Minds, Brains & Machines
description: Prerequisite: L&I 200 (or wavier) or GSTR 210 An introduction to the philosophy of mind, examining such questions as whether there could be consciousness after bodily death, whether the mind is anything 'over and above' the brain and central nervous system, whether a human-built machine could ever think or be conscious. Attention to classic texts and contemporary philosophic research, writing, and rigorous thinking about the nature of mind and thought.

title: Kant & Philosophical Legacies
description: Prerequisite: One PHI course An examination of selected philosophic topics, themes, or movements in 19th- and 20th-century European and American philosophy, such as German idealism, American pragmatism, philosophy as analysis, phenomenology and existentialism, and post-modernism. May be repeated for credit, provided topics differ.

title: Bodies and Sexuality (WGS)
description: An introduction to the philosophy of the body and its relation to sex and sexuality, race, gender, and disability. The course considers historical and contemporary conceptualizations of embodiment and the body in philosophical and social settings, considering issues of embodied and conceptions of the body; the exercise of state and social power over bodies; bodiesâ relation to sexuality, power, and sexual ethics; and bodies in sports.

title: History of Cosmology (HIS)
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 This course will examine the scientific, historical, and philosophical foundations of our understanding of the universe from antiquity to the present. Emphasis will be on developing an appreciation for the science of cosmology through Western traditions and thought. Lecture and discussion will be supplemented with group activities designed to illustrate concepts and practice scientific reasoning skills. By the end of the course, students will have gained an appreciation and understanding of the historical, philosophical, and scientific milestones in scientific cosmology. Non-credit for students who completed it as PHY 186. NonLab Natural Science Course

title: South Asian Phil (AST, REL)
description: An introduction to the philosophical traditions of Vedic ("Hindu") and Non-Vedic (Buddhist and Jain) traditions of South Asia. Students will be introduced to some of the major schools of thought, central primary texts, important thinkers, and contemporary philosophical discussions through exploring and reflecting upon the debates between Vedic and Non-Vedic philosophers on topics such as the nature of the self, theories of consciousness, metaphysics, theories of knowledge, and philosophy of language. This is a reading and writing intensive course.

title: Islamic Philosophy (REL)
description: An introduction to major themes, thinkers, and texts of the Islamic intellectual tradition from the 7th century CE (1st century AH) to the present. Many of the debates engendered by philosophers and theologians more than a thousand years ago still resonate in Muslim philosophical discourse today. The course will end by looking at contemporary issues in Islamic philosophy.

title: Introduction to Engineering
description: Prerequisites: MAT 125 (or waiver), or concurrent enrollment OR permission of instructor This course introduces students to the engineering profession including applications of mathematics methods to physics and engineering, the engineering design process, communication, and ethics. The emphasis is on critical thinking and problem-solving skills that will prepare students for upper-level physics and engineering coursework. Students will also explore the 14 grand engineering challenges of the 21st century. Four hours of lecture each week.

title: General Physics I
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 115 The first semester of a two-semester introductory sequence in non-calculus physics, with laboratory. Major topics may include Newtonian mechanics, conservation of energy and momentum, rotational motion, fluids, and thermodynamics. Students will learn to apply basic principles of physics to explain and model natural phenomena, with an emphasis on applications in the life sciences. Three lecture periods and one laboratory session each week. Natural Science Laboratory Course.

title: Modern Physics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 222 and MAT 135 (or waiver); OR permission of instructor This course provides an introduction to topics in 20th century physics including relativity, introductory quantum mechanics, and atomic and nuclear physics. Other topics of interest may be covered as time allows. Three lecture periods and one laboratory each week.

title: Modern Physics II
description: Prerequisite: PHY 320; OR permission of instructor A continuation of PHY 320 Modern Physics. An introduction to applications and advanced topics in modern physics. Basic principles of quantum mechanics and relativity will be applied to the study of atomic and subatomic physics. Topics may include atomic and molecular spectra, quantum statistics, lasers, electrical conductivity, superconductivity, magnetism, nuclear models and reactions, radioactivity, elementary particles, and cosmology. Four hours of lectures each week. Noncredit for students who completed it as PHY 386 (Fall 2012).

title: Philosophy Seminar I
description: Prerequisite: One 300-level PHI course or permission of instructor Advanced study in a philosophic area, with an emphasis on careful reading of primary texts. Specific topic and prerequisites announced in the Schedule of Classes prior to registration.

title: Optics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 222 or permission of instructor; AND MAT 225 An introduction to optical science, including geometrical and physical optics. Topics include the propagation and measurement of light; geometric optics (Fermat's principle, Snell's law, thin and thick lenses, ray tracing and optical instrumentation); and wave optics (polarization, coherence, diffraction gratings, and interferometry). This is an intermediate Optics course with emphasis on applying theoretical principles in a hands-on setting. Three lecture periods and one laboratory each week.

title: Philosophy Seminar II
description: Prerequisite: PHI 380 Advanced study in a philosophic area, with an emphasis on careful reading of primary texts. Specific topic and prerequisites announced in the Schedule of Classes prior to registration.

title: General Physics II
description: Prerequisite: PHY 127; completion or waiver of MAT 125 or  permission of Instructor The second semester of a two-semester introductory sequence in non-calculus physics, with laboratory. Major topics include oscillations and waves; electricity, magnetism and DC circuits; light and optics; and atomic physics. Students will learn to apply basic principles of physics to explain and model natural phenomena, with an emphasis on applications in the life sciences. Three lectures periods and one laboratory session each week.

title: Biophysics
description: Prerequisite: Completion of PHY 128 or PHY 222 This course introduces students to the application of physics to the study of biological systems, including anatomy, physiology, and molecular biology. Topics to be explored include locomotion, hearing, vision, blood flow, neural conduction, and protein structure. The course also examines the physics of biological and medical research instruments such as microscopes, ultrasound, computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and PET imaging.

title: Intro Physics I with Calculus
description: Prerequisite: MAT 135 (or waiver) or concurrent enrollment This is the first half of a two-course sequence in classical physics with calculus. The focus of the first term is classical mechanics, with emphasis on the relationship between force, motion, and energy. Topics to be covered include kinematics, Newtonian dynamics, gravitation, conservation of momentum and energy, and rotational motion. Three lecture periods and one laboratory each week. Natural Science Laboratory Course.

title: Introduction to Astronomy
description: Prerequisite: Completion or waiver of MAT 012 This course will explore topics in modern astronomy, including the formation of the solar system, the lives and deaths of stars, the structure of the galaxy, and the origin and evolution of the universe. Both lecture and lab will emphasize a hands-on approach to understanding the underlying physical principles that govern astronomy. Three lecture periods and one laboratory each week. Natural Science Laboratory Course.

title: Thermal Physics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 221 and MAT 225; OR permission of instructor An intermediate course covering classical thermodynamics and the methods of statistical mechanics. Topics covered include the first and second laws of thermodynamics with applications, statistical theory of entropy, and classical and quantum ensembles. Four one-hour lectures each week.

title: Intro Physics II with Calculus
description: Prerequisites: PHY 221 and MAT 225, or concurrent enrollment A continuation of the introductory sequence in classical physics with calculus. The second term will focus on the phenomena of electromagnetism and waves. Topics include wave motion and interference, electrostatics, electric and magnetic fields, fundamentals of DC circuits, and electromagnetism. Three lecture periods and one laboratory each week.

title: Quantum Physics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 320 and 481; OR permission of instructor An intermediate course in quantum mechanics, with applications. Topics include SchrÃ¶dinger's equation, particle in a box, the harmonic oscillator, the hydrogen atom, perturbation theory, and the WKB approximation. Four one-hour lectures each week.

title: Intro to Study of Politics
description: A broad survey of the major questions and principles involved in the study of politics.

title: His & Pol of Arab-Isr (HIS)
description: An overview of the Conflict, from the late 19th century to the present.  This course covers the Zionist Movement, the Great Powers and the Ottoman Empire in World War I, the British Mandate for Palestine, the War of 1948, the Suez Canal Crisis, the Six-Day War, the Yom Kippur / 10th of Ramadan War, Camp David Peace Accords, the First Intifada, the Madrid Peace Talks, the Oslo Accords, the Second Intifada.  Other subjects include human rights, international law, refugees, water, boarders, international relations, the role of the USA, and Israeli and Arab political ideologies, leaders and parties.

title: Electromagnetic Theory
description: Prerequisites: PHY 222 and MAT 330; OR permission of instructor An introduction to the fundamentals of classical electricity and magnetism and electromagnetic waves. Emphasizing vector analysis and the laws of Coulomb, Faraday, and Ampere, leading to the development of Maxwell's Theory. Four one-hour lectures each week.

title: Amer Political Thought(HIS)
description: A study of major theoretical traditions related to the design and history of American government and political culture.

title: Advanced General Laboratory
description: Prerequisite: PHY 222 A laboratory course designed to give the student hands-on experience with important experimental techniques in modern physics.  Topics may include vacuum systems; electronic design; experiments in contemporary physics; and independent research projects. One three-hour laboratory session each week. May be repeated for credit for sequential or differing learning experiences.

title: Physics Seminar
description: Prerequisite: Senior standing or permission of instructor A course centered around readings, experiments, and research on topics in physics approved by program faculty.  The seminar emphasizes independent research conducted under the guidance of a faculty member, leading to a substantive research paper and presentation of research results to faculty and students in the program.  Normally taken in the senior year, it is open to other advanced students with permission.

title: Classical Mechanics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 221 and MAT 330 A course covering topics in Newtonian and Lagrangian Mechanics. Topics include rectilinear motion, oscillations, three-dimensional motion, central forces, generalized coordinates and momenta, and Lagrange multipliers. Four one-hour lectures each week.

title: Women Afr Amer-Pol (PSJ/WGS)
description: Prerequisite: GSTR 210 A study of the participation of women and African Americans in the American political process. Theories of representative democracy and an introduction to the historical struggles for equal rights provide a context for the investigation of contemporary electoral politics, governance, grassroots politics, and public policy. Students examine the progress of women and African American candidates, and of related public-policy issues, throughout the current election and its immediate aftermath. African Americans', Appalachians', and Women's Perspective. NOTE: In order to receive African and African American Studies or Women's Studies major or minor credit, the student's major project must focus on African Americans or women, respectively. Active Learning Experience-Service Learning.

title: Materials Physics
description: Prerequisites: PHY 320 and MAT 225 or permission of instructor An introduction to the synthesis, structure, properties, and application of solid materials, including applications to microelectronics, polymers, and energy technology. In particular, the course explores how the crystalline and molecular structure of materials can be related to electronic, optical, thermal, and mechanical properties. Four one-hour lectures each week.

title: Fdn Comm & Civic Eng (PSJ/SOC)
description: An introduction to civic engagement and social change for students interested in applying knowledge from their academic disciplines to contemporary issues and community contexts. The course examines trends in civic engagement over time, including forms it has taken and problems and potential associated with these. Approaches to community and civic engagement are studied through the lens of personal and place-based narratives and case studies at the local, state, and national levels. Through applied projects, students will engage with community leaders and organizations involved in place-based development, outreach, and change. Students will also explore and reflect upon the intersections of self, organizations, and communities. While the course will emphasize civic engagement examples and themes based in the United States, it offers a framework for considering connections with international contexts and communities. Noncredit for students who completed it as GST 286 or SOC 286.

title: American Government
description: An analysis of the basic structures, processes, and contemporary problems of American government.

title: International Relations
description: Prerequisite: PSC 100 or permission of instructor A study of the various forces, assumptions, considerations, and actors that define national interests, shape international relations, and promote world order.

title: The Judicial Process
description: Prerequisite: PSC 110 or permission of instructor The organization, functioning, and political role of the courts and the legal process in the United States.
